entries.intro: The new tax collector near {subject_focus_the} has been taking more than his share. Everyone knows it, but nobody's willing to say it to the right people.
details: He marks down one number in his ledger and collects a different amount. Seen it myself twice now.
The surplus doesn't go to the treasury. He's got a private lockbox he keeps under guard at the inn.
He hired two men from out of town to watch his door. They don't talk to anyone and they don't look friendly.
reactions: The baker tried to confront him and found her permit to sell bread suddenly needed "renewal." Funny how that works.
I'm not brave enough to say it louder than a whisper. Last person who complained found their stall shut down.

statCheck.pass: I did some quiet asking around. His appointment came with a letter from someone high up, but the seal on the letter was wrong: slightly different from the real one.
statCheck.fail: His guards keep a tight circle. Believe me, others have tried to get a look at those ledgers.

entries.intro: The council near {subject_focus_the} voted to redirect the irrigation channel last month. Funny thing: it now waters the chairman's fields first and everyone else gets what's left.
details: Three families downstream have had their crops wilt. The chairman's wheat is the tallest it's ever been.
The vote was five to two. The three who swung it all got new fences built the week after. Paid for by nobody in particular, apparently.
The channel used to split evenly at the fork. Someone dug out the chairman's side wider and packed stones into the other branch to narrow it. Professional work, done overnight.
reactions: It's not even subtle. That's the part that gets me: they don't bother hiding it because they don't think anyone will push back.
The downstream families are talking about pooling coin to dig their own channel. Shouldn't have to come to that.

entries.intro: There's a merchant near {subject_focus_the} who's been buying up land from families in debt. Offering a fraction of what it's worth and pressuring people to take it.
details: He targets anyone who owes money to the mill or the grain store. Waits until they're desperate, then makes his offer.
Three families sold this season alone. He's got more acreage now than the village commons.
I heard he's got an arrangement with the grain store: they call in debts early when he picks a target.
reactions: The families don't talk about it. They're ashamed, and he knows that keeps them quiet.
I tried to warn the last family before they signed. The merchant sent someone to have a word with me. Polite, but the message was clear.

statCheck.pass: I got hold of one of the sale contracts. There's a clause buried at the bottom: the merchant gets mineral rights too. He's not after the farmland. He's after what's underneath it.
statCheck.fail: The families who sold won't share the details. Too embarrassed or too scared, I think.

entries.intro: The guard captain near {subject_focus_the} has been looking the other way when certain wagons come through at night. Wagons that don't stop at the checkpoint.
details: They come once a week, always after midnight, always the same two covered wagons. The guards on duty suddenly find reasons to be elsewhere.
One of the younger guards asked about it and got reassigned to latrine duty for a month. Nobody's asked since.
The wagon wheels leave deep ruts in the road, deeper than any normal cargo would. Whatever they're hauling is heavy, and they're hauling a lot of it.
reactions: I don't know what's in those wagons, but if it were legitimate, they'd come through during the day like everyone else.
The captain's living better than his salary explains. New boots, new cloak, and he's drinking at the expensive tavern now.

statCheck.pass: I followed one of the wagons at a distance once. It went to a warehouse at the edge of town that's supposed to be empty. It was not empty.
statCheck.fail: I have tried staying up to spot them myself. Either they do not come every night or I doze off at the wrong time. Hard to catch ghosts.

entries.intro: The healer near {subject_focus_the} used to treat everyone for what they could afford. Then someone from the capital visited, and now she charges triple and only sees certain people.
details: The visitor left her a supply of rare herbs. The kind you can't get without connections. Now she's dependent on his shipments.
She won't treat anyone who's spoken against the trade guild. It's not official policy: she just always seems to be busy when they come by.
She looks tired. Not greedy: tired. Like she's doing something she doesn't want to do.
reactions: I don't blame her entirely. When someone has leverage over your livelihood, your choices narrow fast.
The people who can't afford her now are going without treatment. A child broke his arm last week and the family had to set it themselves.

entries.intro: The council votes near {subject_focus_the} have been odd lately. Motions nobody asked for pass unanimously, and proposals that half the village supports get struck down before they reach the floor.
details: There was a motion to open a second well on the east side. Everyone wanted it. The council chair ruled it "premature" without a vote.
Meanwhile, a resolution granting exclusive market stalls to three families passed five to nothing. Nobody remembers who proposed it.
The minutes from the last three meetings are missing from the public ledger. The clerk says they were damaged by water, but the ledger looks dry to me.
reactions: It used to feel like our council. Now it feels like their council and we just happen to live here.
I brought it up to a councilwoman and she changed the subject so fast I nearly got dizzy. Whatever is going on, she's either part of it or afraid of it.

statCheck.pass: You're sharp to ask about the timing. All of these strange votes started after the council chair's brother moved back to town. The brother has no official role, but he attends every closed session.
statCheck.fail: Council proceedings are public in theory, but the closed sessions have gotten longer and more frequent. I can't tell you what happens behind those doors.

entries.intro: Someone near {subject_focus_the} has been hoarding grain during the shortage. The public stores are nearly empty, but one warehouse stays locked and full.
details: The warehouse belongs to a trader who sits on the provisions committee. The committee sets distribution amounts, and they've been cutting rations for three weeks.
A woman tried to buy grain directly from him last week. He quoted her four times the normal price and called it a favor.
The guards patrol that warehouse more frequently than the granary itself. Think about what that says.
reactions: People are thinning their porridge and pretending they're not hungry. And this man has sacks stacked to the rafters.
If it gets worse, I don't think locks and guards will stop what happens next. Hunger makes people brave in ways they wouldn't choose.

statCheck.pass: I noticed the grain sacks in his warehouse have a stamp from the communal harvest. Those are supposed to be public stores. He didn't buy that grain: he diverted it.
statCheck.fail: The warehouse has no windows and only one door. Whatever is in there, he's made sure nobody gets a clear look.

entries.intro: The gate guards near {subject_focus_the} have started charging an unofficial toll. If you don't pay, your inspection takes all day. If you do pay, you're through in minutes.
details: It's always the same two guards on the morning shift. They pull traders aside, mention "processing delays," and a coin or two makes the delay vanish.
A farmer told me they held his cart for five hours over a missing stamp that turned out to exist. He paid the fee the next time without being asked.
The captain knows. He has to know. But the morning shift brings in the least complaints, so officially everything is working.
reactions: It's a small amount each time, and that's what makes it work. Nobody wants to make a scene over a few coins. But it adds up.
The honest guards on the other shifts are frustrated. They do their jobs properly and get treated the same as the ones shaking people down.

statCheck.pass: I kept count for a month. The morning shift processes half as many carts but reports no delays. The numbers don't lie: they're throttling traffic on purpose to create demand for the shortcut.
statCheck.fail: It's all done with a nod and a quiet word. Nothing written, nothing witnessed by anyone who would talk. Very tidy.

entries.intro: The elder near {subject_focus_the} has been shutting down anyone who questions the new trade agreement. Not with arguments: with consequences.
details: A potter spoke against the agreement at the last assembly. Within a week his kiln permit was revoked for a code violation nobody had heard of before.
The blacksmith asked to see the full text of the agreement. She was told it's only available for review by council members. Since when?
The elder's son has been visiting people who signed the petition against it. Friendly visits, he says. But the petition has lost half its names since those visits started.
reactions: I signed that petition. I haven't been visited yet, but I sleep lighter now.
Dissent used to be tolerated here. Maybe not welcomed, but tolerated. Now it has a price tag.

statCheck.pass: You can read people, I'll give you that. The elder isn't acting alone. He has a backer outside town who benefits from this agreement. The elder gets a cut: I've seen the courier who brings it.
statCheck.fail: The elder's been careful. He never threatens anyone directly. Everything happens through channels, through process, through timing. Nothing you could point to and call proof.

entries.intro: Someone near {subject_focus_the} has been tampering with the public records. Land boundaries, harvest tallies, water rights: small changes, but they always favor the same people.
details: A family checked their deed last month and found their eastern boundary had shifted ten paces on the official map. It used to run to the stream. Now it stops short.
The harvest tallies for two seasons ago were rewritten. The original numbers don't match what's in the ledger now, and nobody authorized the change.
The clerk's handwriting is on all the amended documents. But the clerk swears she copied them from originals that were already changed when she received them.
reactions: Records are supposed to be the one thing you can trust. If the records lie, then nothing is settled.
The people benefiting from the changes all have connections to the surveyor. That might be coincidence, but I stopped believing in coincidence about three boundary changes ago.

statCheck.pass: I compared the ink on the amendments to the ink in the rest of the ledger. Different shade, different consistency. Those changes were made well after the original entries, not at the same time.
statCheck.fail: The records room is locked most days and only the clerk and the council chair have keys. I haven't been able to examine them closely enough to see what's been changed.

entries.intro: The bridge repair fund near {subject_focus_the} collected a full chest of coin last season. The bridge is still broken and nobody can say where the money went.
details: The treasurer gave a report at the last assembly. According to him, the funds were spent on materials. But no materials were ever delivered.
I asked the mason who was supposed to do the work. He said he was never hired. Never even approached.
The treasurer has a new cellar being dug behind his house. Hired laborers from two towns over to do it. Doesn't want local hands, apparently.
reactions: People gave what they could spare because that bridge matters. Farmers, fishers, tradespeople: everyone put in. And now they're being told the money is gone with nothing to show for it.
Nobody wants to accuse the treasurer directly. He's been here his whole life. But good people can do bad things when the opportunity is easy enough.

statCheck.pass: You know what's interesting? The treasurer's report mentions a supplier by name. That supplier doesn't exist. I checked with every material dealer within three days' travel. Nobody has heard of them.
statCheck.fail: The financial records are the treasurer's to keep, and he doesn't share them outside assembly reports. Whatever the truth is, he's the only one with the full picture.

entries.intro: There's a family near {subject_focus_the} that always gets first pick of the best market stalls, the best plots in the commons, the best seats at council. And nobody elected them to anything.
details: Their patriarch lends money to half the village. Not a lot: just enough that people owe him something. And when people owe him something, they tend to agree with him.
The market steward assigns stalls. The steward owes the patriarch for his daughter's wedding feast. Funny how the best corner stall is always available for the family.
Anyone who's crossed them finds their life getting quietly harder. Deliveries delayed, permits stalled, invitations lost. Nothing dramatic. Just friction.
reactions: It's not the kind of power that announces itself. It's the kind that whispers, and everyone adjusts without being told.
I used to think they were just well-liked. Now I think people are just careful around them. Those look the same from a distance.
The worst part is they're not even bad people individually. It's the system they've built around themselves that's rotten.

statCheck.pass: You see it too? Watch who agrees with the patriarch at assembly and then count who owes him money. The overlap is exact. Every vote goes his way because half the room is in his ledger.
statCheck.fail: It all looks perfectly normal on the surface. Favors between neighbors, debts between friends. You'd have to live here for years to see the pattern.

entries.intro: A trader near {subject_focus_the} has been spreading rumors about her competitors. Every season a different rival gets accused of selling spoiled goods or cheating on weight, and every season that rival loses their customers.
details: The dyer lost half his business after word spread that he was using cheap pigment. I tested his dye myself: it's fine. Better than fine.
The leather seller before him got accused of selling thinned hides. She left town out of shame. A month later, the trader expanded into leather goods.
The accusations never come from the trader directly. They start with people who owe her favors, spread through the market, and by the time anyone checks, the damage is done.
reactions: Reputation is everything at market. One bad rumor can undo years of honest work. She knows that and she uses it.
I'm half expecting to be next. I've been outselling her on candles for two months now.

statCheck.pass: I traced the last rumor back to its source. It started with a woman who works for the trader's household. When I confronted her, she went pale and said she was told to say it. Wouldn't say by whom, but we both know.
statCheck.fail: Rumors don't leave footprints. By the time you hear one, it's been through four mouths and nobody remembers where it started.

entries.intro: The labor crews near {subject_focus_the} are being worked harder and paid less than what the contracts say. The foreman pockets the difference and dares anyone to complain.
details: The contract says six hours of work for a full day's wage. The foreman keeps them for nine and writes down six. Says the extra time is for setup and cleanup, which isn't paid work.
A stonemason refused the extra hours last month. He was replaced the next day. The foreman brought in someone from outside who'd work for even less.
The workers talk about it at the tavern but won't say a word near the worksite. The foreman has a man who watches and listens.
reactions: These are people with families. They take the abuse because the next meal depends on it. That's not loyalty: that's a trap.
I told one of the workers to bring it to the council. He laughed. Said the foreman's cousin sits on the council. Same blood, same pocket.

statCheck.pass: I got a look at the original contract and the foreman's work log. The hours don't match, and neither do the pay amounts. He's shaving a quarter of the wages off the top and recording it as material costs.
statCheck.fail: The foreman keeps his records close and his workers closer. Nobody wants to risk their position by talking to outsiders about the details.

entries.intro: The orphan fund near {subject_focus_the} was supposed to build a shelter for the children who lost parents in the flood. The fund is empty now, and the only thing built is an extension on the alderman's house.
details: The alderman managed the fund personally. He gave reports at assembly, but the numbers were always round and vague. No receipts, no invoices.
The children are still sleeping in the temple hall. The temple keeper has been feeding them out of her own stores, and she's running thin.

Someone asked the alderman directly at the last assembly. He said construction costs exceeded estimates and the remaining funds went to administrative expenses. He did not say what those expenses were.
reactions: People donated to help children. Not to pay for a new dining room in the alderman's house. The anger is quiet now, but it's there.
The temple keeper won't accuse him. She says it would divide the village and the children would suffer more. She might be right, but the silence is eating her alive.

statCheck.pass: You're good at reading between the lines. The alderman's extension used the same timber supplier that was listed in the orphan fund's expenses. Same wood, same quantities, same delivery date. He built his house with their money.
statCheck.fail: The alderman's records are his own, and he answers questions at assembly with just enough detail to avoid suspicion. Without access to his books, it's all circumstantial.

entries.intro: There's a protection arrangement near {subject_focus_the} that nobody talks about openly. A group of men visit the market stalls weekly, and the stalls that don't contribute to their "community fund" seem to have the worst luck.
details: The cobbler refused to pay last season. The next week, someone broke his display rack overnight. No witnesses, no suspects.
They call it a community fund, but there's no record of where the coin goes. No projects, no improvements, no accountability.
The leader of the group used to work for the guard. He knows exactly how patrols run and when certain streets are unwatched.
reactions: Everyone pays because the alternative is worse. That's not a community fund: that's a threat wearing a polite name.
The guards know. I'm almost certain. But the leader has friends among them, and complaints have a way of going nowhere.

statCheck.pass: I noticed the men skip certain stalls entirely. The ones they skip? All connected to the same family on the council. That family is either running this or being paid to look away.
statCheck.fail: They're careful about who they talk to and when. The whole thing runs on nods and quiet words. Nothing you could take to anyone official.

entries.intro: The well master near {subject_focus_the} controls who draws water and when. It used to be open access. Now there's a schedule, and the schedule favors those who bring him gifts.
details: Families who leave a basket at his door get the morning slot when the water is cleanest and coolest. Everyone else draws in the afternoon heat.
He justifies the schedule by saying the well needs rest periods to refill. I've drawn from that well my entire life: it has never needed rest.
A widow with three children was moved to the last slot of the day. By the time she draws, the bucket comes up half mud. She can't afford the baskets he expects.
reactions: Water isn't a luxury. It's survival. And he's turned it into a currency for his own comfort.
Some of the men want to dig a new well. The well master convinced the council it would weaken the water table. Convenient opinion from the man who profits from scarcity.

statCheck.pass: Here's something most people don't realize: the well master's appointment was supposed to be temporary. The original charter says the position rotates yearly. He's been in it for six years. Nobody's enforced the rotation because nobody remembers the rule.
statCheck.fail: He runs that well like it's his personal property, and nobody with the authority to overrule him seems interested in doing so. The system protects its own.

entries.intro: Someone near {subject_focus_the} has been filing false accusations against families to seize their property. Three homesteads have been claimed by the council for "unpaid obligations" that the families say don't exist.
details: The first family was told they owed a tax from two years ago that they'd already paid. They showed a receipt, but the council said the receipt didn't match their records.
The second family was accused of encroaching on public land. Their fence has been in the same place for a generation. The boundary marker was moved.
All three properties were auctioned within days of seizure. The same buyer won every auction. He's not from here.
reactions: These families built their lives on that land. Some of them were born in those houses. And now they're standing in the road with nothing.
I'm keeping my receipts for everything now. Every payment, every tax, every transaction. If they come for me, I'll have proof. I hope.

statCheck.pass: The buyer at those auctions? He's the brother-in-law of the council clerk who processed the seizure orders. They're not even trying to hide the connection if you know where to look.
statCheck.fail: The seizure orders look legitimate on paper. Proper stamps, proper signatures. If there's fraud, it's buried deep in procedure where most people wouldn't think to look.

entries.intro: The trade guild near {subject_focus_the} held elections last month. The results were announced before half the members had finished voting.
details: Ballots were collected at the guild hall, but the counting was done in a back room with only two witnesses. Both witnesses are on the winner's payroll.
Three members who planned to vote against the incumbent arrived to find their names struck from the membership rolls. Clerical error, they were told.
The winner has run the guild for twelve years. Every election, someone raises concerns. Every election, the concerns are noted and nothing changes.
reactions: A guild is supposed to represent its members. This one represents its leader and everyone else pays dues for the privilege of being ignored.
I stopped attending guild meetings. Half the room is afraid to speak and the other half is paid to clap.

statCheck.pass: One of the struck members kept her original enrollment receipt. It's dated, signed, and stamped. Her removal from the rolls happened the day before the election: not a clerical error. A deliberate act.
statCheck.fail: Guild records are internal. Unless you're a member in good standing, you don't get to see the rolls, the ballots, or the minutes. And good standing is whatever the guild leader says it is.

entries.intro: There's an informant near {subject_focus_the} who reports to interests outside the settlement. People's private business has been showing up in places it shouldn't.
details: A merchant negotiated a supply deal in confidence. Two days later, a competitor from the next town over made an identical offer at a lower price. That kind of timing isn't luck.
The blacksmith mentioned a new alloy technique to three people. Within a week, an outside guild sent a letter asking about licensing it. She hadn't told anyone outside those three.
People have started being careful about what they say and to whom. Conversations at the tavern have gotten shorter and quieter.
reactions: It's a terrible feeling: not knowing who in your own village is selling your words to strangers.
Trust holds a community together. Someone is pulling out the threads, and we can't even see whose hand is doing it.

statCheck.pass: I started feeding different information to different people to see where it surfaced. Only one version made it outside: the one I told to the courier's assistant. She's been passing messages both directions.
statCheck.fail: I've been watching, but whoever it is, they're careful. The leaks keep happening and I can't narrow it down. Everyone looks guilty when you're suspicious enough.

entries.intro: The road warden near {subject_focus_the} has been collecting tolls that don't appear in any official schedule. Travelers pay because he wears the badge, but the coin never reaches the treasury.
details: He stations himself at the narrowest point of the road where carts can't easily turn around. You either pay or you wait while he inspects every nail in your wagon.
A carter from the next valley questioned the toll and was detained for half a day over a supposed cargo discrepancy. The cargo was flour.
I spoke to the treasury clerk. She says the road warden's post was defunded two years ago. He's not even supposed to be collecting.
reactions: He's got the uniform and the authority to make your day miserable. That's all you need to run a toll that doesn't exist.
The traders have started going around by the longer route. It adds half a day, but at least nobody shakes them down.

statCheck.pass: I checked the appointment records. His position was terminated, but nobody revoked his badge or his authority seal. He's operating on paperwork that should have been collected two years ago. Anyone with the authority to stop him either doesn't know or doesn't care.
statCheck.fail: He has a badge and he has a post. From the outside, it all looks official. You'd have to dig through the administrative records to see that his authority expired, and nobody bothers with that.

entries.intro: The magistrate near {subject_focus_the} has been settling disputes in favor of whoever brings the larger gift to his office the day before the hearing.
details: A farmer lost a boundary dispute to his neighbor last month. The neighbor brought the magistrate a cask of aged wine the evening before. The farmer brought his case on its merits.
Three rulings in a row have gone against the poorer party. Not unusual on its own, but the gifts arriving the night before each ruling: that's a pattern.
The magistrate's clerk keeps a separate log of visitors. I've seen him writing in it. It never appears in the official record of proceedings.
reactions: Justice for sale. It's the oldest corruption there is, and it still works because the people who can afford it don't complain and the people who can't are too powerless to be heard.
I have a dispute coming before him next season. I'm already dreading it. I can't afford to compete with gifts, and I can't afford to lose.

statCheck.pass: You're perceptive. The clerk's visitor log: I got a glimpse of it once. Every ruling correlates with a visit the night before from the winning party. Every single one. That's not coincidence. That's a price list.
statCheck.fail: The magistrate is careful to justify every ruling with proper precedent. On paper, each decision looks sound. You'd need access to records he keeps very private to prove otherwise.

entries.intro: A woman near {subject_focus_the} has been blackmailing people with secrets she shouldn't know. Old debts, past mistakes, things people thought were buried.
details: She approached the tanner last month about a debt he defaulted on years ago in another town. Told him she'd make it public unless he voted her way at the next assembly.
The miller's wife was confronted about something from before her marriage. She came home in tears and hasn't spoken at assembly since.
Nobody knows how she's getting the information. These aren't local secrets. They're things from other places, other lives, things people came here to leave behind.
reactions: Everyone has something they'd rather keep quiet. She's turned that into a weapon, and the people she's using it on can't fight back without exposing the very thing they're trying to hide.
The assembly has gotten very agreeable lately. Unanimous votes on everything. That should worry people more than it does.

statCheck.pass: I noticed she receives a sealed letter every few weeks from a courier who doesn't stop anywhere else in town. Someone outside is feeding her the information. She's not the source: she's the instrument.
statCheck.fail: She keeps her leverage private and her methods quiet. The people she's pressuring won't talk about it, and she certainly won't. It's a closed circle.

entries.intro: Witnesses near {subject_focus_the} have been pressured to change their stories before disputes reach the council. People who saw something start remembering differently after a certain visitor comes calling.
details: A woman saw the surveyor moving a boundary marker and reported it. Two days later she told the council she must have been mistaken about what she saw. She wasn't mistaken.
The visitor is always the same man: quiet, polite, never threatening. He just asks people to reconsider what they remember. And then he mentions something about their family.
Three separate testimonies have been retracted this season. All three involved complaints against the same merchant house.
reactions: When witnesses stop telling the truth, the truth stops mattering. That's the point. You don't need everyone to lie: you just need enough doubt.
I was almost a witness myself once. A friend told me to keep quiet for my own good. I did. I'm not proud of it.

statCheck.pass: The man who does the visiting? He's not from here. I followed him after his last visit. He reports to someone at the merchant house directly: walked in the back entrance like he belonged there.
statCheck.fail: The witnesses won't admit they were pressured. They've been made to feel like recanting was their own decision. Without them willing to say otherwise, there's nothing to work with.

entries.intro: The charity stores near {subject_focus_the} have been sending out lighter bundles than what donors contribute. What goes in and what comes out don't match.
details: A woman donated a full sack of grain for the winter distribution. The family that received it got half a sack. The difference went somewhere.
The charity warden blamed rodents for the first shortfall. And the second. By the third time, people stopped asking and he stopped explaining.
His wife has been wearing new clothes that don't come from any local tailor. Fine cloth, well-made. The kind you buy with coin you shouldn't have.
reactions: Charity depends on trust. The people who give need to believe it reaches the people who need it. Once that trust breaks, the giving stops and the needy suffer twice.
I've started giving directly to families instead of through the stores. It's less efficient, but at least I know it arrives.

statCheck.pass: I marked a sack I donated with a scratch on the tie cord. I found that same sack at the warden's own residence, tucked in with his household stores. Not even repackaged. Just taken.
statCheck.fail: The warden controls the inventory log and does the distribution himself. Without someone on the inside willing to compare notes, the numbers he reports are the only numbers anyone sees.

entries.intro: A council member near {subject_focus_the} has been awarding public contracts to her own family's workshop. Every mason, carpenter, and laborer in town submits bids, but the same workshop wins every time.
details: The last three projects went to her brother's crew. Their bids were lower than everyone else's by exactly the right amount, as if someone told them what number to write.
A carpenter submitted a competitive bid for the storehouse repair. He was told his bid arrived too late. He delivered it two days early.
The brother's crew does passable work, but it's not the best in town. It doesn't need to be when the contract is already decided before the bidding starts.
reactions: Honest craftspeople are losing work because the game is rigged before they sit down to play. Some of them are talking about leaving.
I wouldn't mind if the work was good. But the storehouse roof they repaired leaked again within two months. We paid for a fix and got a patch.

statCheck.pass: I compared the winning bids to the actual costs of materials. Her brother's bids are impossibly low: lower than material costs alone. They're underbidding to win, then billing overruns after the fact. The final cost always ends up higher than the second-lowest bid.
statCheck.fail: The bidding records are filed with the council and she's the one who reviews them. Asking to audit her own decisions hasn't gotten anyone very far.

entries.intro: Someone on the provisions committee near {subject_focus_the} has been selling emergency reserves to outside buyers. The stores that are supposed to keep us through a bad season are half what they should be.
details: The last inspection counted forty barrels of salt pork. The inventory report from six months ago listed seventy-two. The committee chair says some were rotted and disposed of. Thirty-two barrels of spoilage is not credible.
A trader from the eastern settlements told me he bought cured provisions from a supplier near here at a good price. The barrels had our settlement's stamp on them.
The committee only has three members, and two of them defer to the chair on everything. There is no real oversight.
reactions: We keep those stores for the lean months. If a hard winter comes and the barrels are empty, people will learn the truth at the worst possible moment.
I tried to raise it at assembly and was told the inventory is a committee matter, not a public one. Since when are our emergency stores not the public's business?

statCheck.pass: I spoke to the carter who hauls disposals to the midden. He says he hasn't picked up spoiled provisions from the stores in over a year. Whatever left those shelves, it didn't go to the dump.
statCheck.fail: The stores are kept locked and the committee does its own inspections. I'm going on secondhand accounts because nobody outside the committee gets to count the barrels.

entries.intro: The new schoolmaster near {subject_focus_the} is teaching the children that certain families are more important than others. It's subtle, but the children are repeating it.
details: He gives the children from connected families special tasks and public praise. The rest get the routine work and silence.
A girl told her mother that the schoolmaster said some families built this village and others just live in it. The mother confronted him. He said the girl misunderstood.
He was appointed by the council chair without the usual process. No other candidates were considered, and his qualifications come from a recommendation letter that nobody has seen.
reactions: Children absorb what they're taught. If they grow up believing some of them matter more, that belief hardens into the way things are. That's how you build the next generation of this problem.
The parents who object are in a difficult position. Pull their children out and they miss the schooling. Keep them in and they absorb the message.

statCheck.pass: I asked around about his previous posting. He was let go from a school two settlements over for the same thing: using the classroom to promote the interests of whoever was paying him. The council chair knew this when he appointed him.
statCheck.fail: He's careful with his words in front of adults. The bias shows in the classroom, but a visitor sitting in wouldn't catch it. You'd have to hear what the children say to each other afterward.

entries.intro: A group of merchants near {subject_focus_the} have carved up the trade routes between them. If you're not part of their arrangement, your goods don't reach market.
details: An independent trader tried to sell his wool at the crossroads market last season. His cart was turned away by men who said the route was spoken for. No law gives them that right.

The merchants set prices together. Nobody competes because the routes are divided: one handles grain, another handles cloth, another handles metals. If you want to sell, you sell through them at their price.
They pay the road wardens enough to report any independent carts heading to market. If a competitor shows up, they know before the competitor arrives.
reactions: A free market requires, at minimum, that it actually be free. This isn't trade: it's a toll booth with extra steps.
The worst part is the prices for us. With no competition, they charge what they want. And what they want is always a little more than last season.

statCheck.pass: I talked to one of the road wardens after a few drinks. He gets paid double his official wage by the merchant group for reporting cart traffic. He feels bad about it, he says, but not bad enough to stop. At least he was honest about that.
statCheck.fail: The arrangement isn't written down anywhere. It's all handshake deals and understood boundaries. You'd need someone on the inside willing to talk, and the profit is good enough to keep everyone quiet.

entries.intro: The night council near {subject_focus_the} is something people whisper about but won't discuss openly. A handful of influential people meet after the official council adjourns, and the real decisions get made then.
details: The official council votes on motions, but the motions always pass or fail exactly the way certain members predicted. The votes are a performance: the outcome was settled the night before.
They meet at the back of the guild hall after the lamps are turned down. I've seen four or five figures go in, and they don't come out until well past midnight.
A junior council member tried to attend once. She was told it was a private supper among friends. Friends who happen to control every significant decision in the settlement.
reactions: We hold elections and debates and assemblies, and none of it matters if the decisions are already made in a dark room by people nobody voted for.
I don't know what scares me more: that it's happening, or that everyone seems to know and nobody does anything about it.

statCheck.pass: I matched the dates of the night meetings with the council agenda. Every contentious vote was preceded by a meeting the night before. And every one of those votes went the same direction: in favor of the merchant families who attend that back room.
statCheck.fail: It's hard to prove a meeting happened when everyone involved says they were home in bed. Without someone who was in that room willing to talk, it's just my suspicion against their word.
